source.title: Characterization of sleep in zebrafish and insomnia in hypocretin receptor mutants

confidence_rationale: Seminal paper establishing zebrafish as a bona fide sleep model. Demonstrates all three canonical sleep criteria (reversible immobility, elevated arousal threshold, homeostatic rebound after deprivation) and place preference during rest. Orexin/hypocretin system present and functional. Confidence high because behavioural and molecular sleep criteria are directly demonstrated; docked slightly for zebrafish-to-REE transfer and for the absence of EEG-level staging data.
failure_signatures: Zebrafish sleep differs from mammalian sleep in that light suppresses rather than promotes sleep, with no rebound after light-induced suppression -- suggesting partial divergence in circadian-homeostatic coupling.
Hypocretin receptor knockout causes fragmented sleep rather than narcolepsy/cataplexy, indicating molecular diversity in sleep regulatory networks across vertebrates.
Absence of electrophysiological staging means SWS vs REM analogs cannot be confirmed from this paper alone.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Adult zebrafish exhibit a sleep state characterised by reversible immobility, increased arousal threshold, place preference, and homeostatic rebound after rest deprivation -- regulated by an orexin/hypocretin system homologous to the mammalian wake-promoting circuit.
mapping.ree_translation: INV-010 asserts that offline integration phases are not optional but structurally required. The zebrafish data establishes that a vertebrate at the approximate complexity level of the REE minimal mind already requires sleep with homeostatic regulation -- it is not an accretion of higher cognition but is present at the simplest vertebrate level. ARC-011 (offline integration required for stability) is directly supported: sleep-deprived zebrafish accumulate sleep pressure that must be discharged, indicating an integrative burden that accumulates during waking and can only be resolved offline.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The zebrafish-to-REE transfer assumes the REE minimal mind is operating at a complexity level roughly equivalent to a teleost vertebrate. This is an architectural assumption, not a confirmed empirical fact. The homeostatic rebound in zebrafish confirms the sleep-drive machinery; it does not specify what cognitive or representational functions are being consolidated.
mapping.source_context: Adult zebrafish behavioural characterisation; rest/activity monitoring; arousal threshold testing; electrical-stimulation rest deprivation with rebound measurement; hypocretin receptor mutant analysis.

confidence_components.notes: Very high source quality (seminal, PLoS Biology, widely replicated). Mapping fidelity strong for the sleep-necessity and homeostatic-drive claims. Transfer risk low -- zebrafish are the proposed calibration model for the REE minimal mind, so the species transfer is the point rather than a liability.
